toxicity_notes: Generally non-toxic for humans and pets; widely eaten fresh/dried as a culinary herb. No significant documented toxicity.

Generally non-toxic; widely used as a culinary/medicinal herb. Large quantities of essential oil can cause gastric irritation, but culinary amounts are safe for humans and regarded as non-toxic to pets.

Generally non-toxic; widely used as a culinary/medicinal herb. High-concentration menthol oil can irritate mucous membranes, but leaf/culinary use is safe and non-toxic for humans; regarded as non-toxic to pets.

Generally non-toxic for adults in culinary amounts; leaves widely used as a seasoning. Essential oil is concentrated and can cause neurotoxicity/seizure-like symptoms in large doses or in children/pets if ingested. Treat concentrated oil as hazardous; normal culinary herb use is safe.

Generally non-toxic in culinary amounts. Thujone in common sage oil is mild and not a concern in food amounts; large doses of concentrated essential oil or tea can be neurotoxic. Generally regarded as safe as a food herb.

Generally non-toxic; leaves widely used culinary herb. Thymol is the primary bioactive and is safe at culinary doses; high-concentration essential oil can irritate mucous membranes. Normal use is safe for humans and pets.
Generally non-toxic; leaves used as culinary herb. Essential oil contains carvacrol/thymol but is safe at culinary doses. No significant documented toxicity for humans in food amounts.
Generally non-toxic; culinary herb. Essential oil in large doses can cause sedation/irritation, but leaf use is safe. Generally regarded as safe for normal culinary use.

Generally non-toxic in small amounts; flowers/leaves used in food/perfume. Lavender oil in large doses may cause GI distress or confusion in humans and can be irritating/toxic to cats/dogs in concentrated form. Culinary sparse use is usual and generally regarded as safe.
Generally non-toxic; leaves widely used in teas and culinary applications. Generally regarded as safe. No significant documented toxicity or pet-specific hazards reported in normal use.

Generally non-toxic to humans; commonly used as mild herbal tea/decoration. Nepetalactone is an attractant for felines, but no significant human toxicity documented. Not known to be hazardous to pets beyond behavioral stimulation.

Leaves are edible and used in East Asian cuisine; some varieties/seeds are consumed safely after cooking. Seeds and some chemotypes may contain perilla ketone/cyanogenic glycosides — raw/unidentified material can cause toxicity in sensitive species. Prefer identified culinary varieties prepared safely.

Not well documented; generally regarded as having low toxicity, but specific formal toxicology data are sparse. Some Salvia species contain thujone; S. splendens is not known as a high-thujone species. No major documented poisoning risk; treat as generally low toxicity pending formal source.
Generally considered non-toxic to humans and widely used as an ornamental. ASPCA classifies Plectranthus as non-toxic. No significant documented toxicity.

Generally non-toxic; used ornamentally. Young leaves have been eaten as a salad green historically. No significant documented toxicity for humans or pets; some reports of mild skin irritation from hairy leaves.

Generally non-toxic; leaves/flowers used in teas and as edible garnish. Some individuals are allergic to Lamiaceae pollen/resins though rare. Foliage can be irritant to pets if large amounts are ingested; regarded generally as non-toxic.

Generally non-toxic; historical medicinal herbal use for respiratory/kidney complaints, also eaten in salads. Raw plants are quite bitter and not typically eaten in volume. Large quantities may cause mild GI irritation. Documented invasive/naturalized in parts of North America.

Generally non-toxic in normal ornamental exposure. Historically used medicinally, but high-doses or concentrated essential oil have been associated with hepatotoxicity in case reports/animal studies; treat as generally safe for horticulture but avoid ingesting concentrated preparations.
Generally non-toxic for humans and pets; used ornamentally. No significant documented toxicity. Young leaves have been eaten in some contexts but not commonly as food.

Generally low toxicity; fruits used medicinally for hormonal conditions. Unsafe in pregnancy due to potential hormonal effects — avoid during pregnancy. Generally considered safe otherwise for adults. No major pet toxicity documented.
